text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic approach in the abdomen, identifying the greater curvature and preserving a large omental skirt for later use as an omental patch. The dissection proceeds into the lesser sac laterally, extending towards the short gastric vessels while avoiding the gastro-epiploic artery and staying close to the gastric wall. The lesser curvature is approached, and the gastro-hepatic ligament is dissected up to the right crus. The surgeon ensures circumferential mobility around the distal esophagus and exposes the left gastric artery, isolating the area by taking down omental attachments. A vascular stapler is used to transect the base of the left gastric artery, ensuring lymph node tissue is included.

The surgeon extends the dissection into the hiatal opening and begins forming the gastric conduit by dividing the lesser curvature vasculature. Multiple staple fires are used to create the conduit, ensuring a 4 cm diameter from the greater curvature wall to the staple line. Retraction is applied to extend the conduit, typically requiring seven to eight staple loads. The tip of the conduit is checked for perfusion, and the specimen is attached to the tip, ensuring the staple line is on the right. Two sutures secure the orientation before the conduit is checked again prior to moving into the thoracic cavity.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral cubitus position. The surgeon dissects the esophagus from the mediastinum, carefully dividing subclinal nodal tissue to keep it with the specimen, performing an en bloc resection. The esophagus is divided from the posterior mediastinum, avoiding injury to the thoracic duct, with branches clipped using harmonic devices. The dissection extends to the thoracic inlet, maintaining a pleural tent above the esophagus vein to cover the anastomosis. The gastric conduit is delivered into the thoracic cavity, ensuring the staple line orientation is maintained, and the suture is divided to separate the specimen from the conduit.

The surgeon checks the mobilization of the esophagus and uses endoshears to sharply divide the proximal esophagus, fully resecting the specimen. The remaining esophagus is prepared for anastomosis. The posterior inferior incision is extended by a few centimeters, and a wound protector is placed. The specimen is delivered through the incision for pathology. Cautery is used to manage any bleeding.

The surgeon examines the end of the esophagus and uses a Foley balloon catheter to dilate it if necessary. An EEA stapler is then inserted into the esophageal opening, and two purse-string sutures are placed to secure the esophagus around the anvil. The first suture is a baseball stitch to snugly fit the mucosa around the anvil, followed by a second exterior purse string to ensure a flat surface for the anastomosis.

The anvil is snugly positioned around the esophagus. The surgeon pulls the gastric conduit further up and divides the proximal staple line to open the conduit. The distal staple line is opened where sutures were placed, creating an opening large enough for the EEA stapler handle. The handle is advanced distally, and the spike is brought out along the greater curve. The anvil is attached, and the esophagus is gently brought down to meet the gastric conduit, forming the anastomosis with the stapler. The NG tube is advanced, and the open end of the conduit is stapled off. The 'candy cane' portion of the conduit is resected, ensuring a centimeter of space between staple lines. The staple line is covered with omentum to form a mantle patch, sutured to the pleura and mediastinum. The chest is irrigated, and chest tubes are placed for drainage, completing the procedure.
